questions.question: What is `effect()` for, and why does the Angular team advise using it sparingly? Give one legitimate use case and one case where `computed()` or template binding is the better tool.
questions.explanation:
Intuition: effect() registers a function that Angular re-runs whenever any signal it read during its last run changes. It is for side effects that leave the Angular world — logging, storage, third-party DOM libs — never for deriving state.

Lifecycle: created in an injection context (constructor or field initializer); runs once immediately-ish (scheduled) — that first run records its dependencies; on any dependency change it is scheduled and re-runs (multiple sets coalesce into one run); it is destroyed automatically with the component, and onCleanup runs before every re-run and on destroy.

export class ThemeComponent {
  theme = signal<'light' | 'dark'>('light');

  constructor() {
    // legitimate: sync a signal OUT of Angular
    effect(() => {
      localStorage.setItem('theme', this.theme()); // runs now, then after each change
    });

    // cleanup: runs before each re-run and on component destroy
    effect((onCleanup) => {
      const id = setInterval(() => console.log(this.theme()), 1000);
      onCleanup(() => clearInterval(id));
    });
  }
}

Anti-pattern — using an effect to derive state:

// BAD: hidden write chain, runs later, can glitch
effect(() => this.fullName.set(this.first() + ' ' + this.last()));

// GOOD: declarative, lazy, memoized
fullName = computed(() => this.first() + ' ' + this.last());

Takeaway: if the effect's body ends in someSignal.set(...), you almost certainly wanted computed() (or linkedSignal()) instead.

questions.question: What problem does `linkedSignal()` solve? Describe a concrete scenario — e.g. a selected item that must reset when the list it belongs to changes — and explain how it differs from `computed()`.
questions.explanation:
Intuition: linkedSignal() is a writable signal whose value is recomputed from a source when that source changes. It fills the gap between signal() (writable, but knows nothing about other state) and computed() (derived, but read-only — you can never set() it).

Scenario: a shipping selector — the user may pick any option, but when the options list changes the selection must snap back to something valid:

options = signal(['standard', 'express', 'overnight']);

// derived like computed, but writable
selected = linkedSignal(() => this.options()[0]);

this.selected.set('overnight');          // user picks one — allowed
this.options.set(['pickup', 'courier']); // source changed -> selected resets to 'pickup'

The long form gets the previous value, so you can keep the selection when it is still valid:

selected = linkedSignal({
  source: this.options,
  computation: (opts, prev) =>
    prev && opts.includes(prev.value) ? prev.value : opts[0],
});

vs computed(): a computed would always show options()[0] and throw away the user's choice — it cannot hold local writes. Takeaway: use linkedSignal() for "local state with a reset rule"; use computed() when the value is purely derived and never set directly.

questions.question: How do signals and RxJS interoperate? Explain `toSignal()` and `toObservable()`, including what initial value problem `toSignal()` has to deal with and where subscription/cleanup happens.
questions.explanation:
Intuition: a signal must always hold a current value you can read synchronously; an observable is a stream that may emit later (or never). toSignal() bridges this by subscribing immediately and caching the latest emission in a signal; toObservable() watches a signal with an effect and emits each new value.

count$ = interval(1000);

// subscribes RIGHT NOW (eager!), caches latest value
count = toSignal(this.count$);                      // Signal<number | undefined>
count0 = toSignal(this.count$, { initialValue: 0 }); // Signal<number>
// requireSync: obs MUST emit during subscribe (BehaviorSubject does), else it throws
user = toSignal(this.user$, { requireSync: true });

The initial-value problem: between subscribing and the first emission the signal still has to return something — hence undefined by default, or your initialValue, or requireSync: true when the source emits synchronously. If the observable errors, reading the signal rethrows that error.

query = signal('');
query$ = toObservable(this.query); // emits via an effect

this.query.set('a'); this.query.set('ab'); this.query.set('abc');
// effect runs once after the tick -> subscriber sees only 'abc'

Subscription & cleanup: both must be called in an injection context (constructor / field initializer) or given an Injector. When that injector is destroyed, toSignal unsubscribes and toObservable completes — no manual teardown.

Takeaway: toSignal = eager subscribe + cache latest; toObservable = effect-driven, so synchronous bursts coalesce to the last value.

questions.question: What is the difference between a process and a thread, and what does a `Thread` in Java map to at the OS level? Why is creating platform threads expensive, and what are the standard ways to start work on another thread?
questions.explanation:
A process has its own memory space; threads live inside a process and share its heap, which is why they can exchange data directly (and why they can race). A Java platform Thread wraps one OS thread 1:1 — the kernel schedules it. It is expensive because creation is a syscall, each thread reserves a big fixed stack (~1 MB), and the kernel must context-switch between them.

Runnable task = () -> System.out.println("work");

new Thread(task).start();                    // 1. raw platform thread — rare in real code

ExecutorService pool = Executors.newFixedThreadPool(4);
pool.submit(task);                           // 2. thread pool — the standard way

Thread.startVirtualThread(task);             // 3. virtual thread (Java 21+) — cheap, not 1:1

Takeaway: platform threads are a scarce OS resource, so you pool them; virtual threads remove that scarcity by multiplexing many virtual threads onto few OS threads.

questions.question: How does `BlockingQueue` support the producer–consumer pattern? Compare `ArrayBlockingQueue`, `LinkedBlockingQueue` and `SynchronousQueue`, and explain what backpressure means in this context and how a bounded queue provides it.
questions.explanation:
A BlockingQueue is the classic producer–consumer channel: put() blocks while the queue is full, take() blocks while it's empty — all the wait/notify plumbing is inside the queue, so producers and consumers stay trivially simple.

BlockingQueue<Job> q = new ArrayBlockingQueue<>(100);
// producer:  q.put(job);   // blocks when 100 jobs are waiting
// consumer:  Job j = q.take(); // blocks when empty
  • ArrayBlockingQueue — fixed-size array, always bounded, one lock for both ends; predictable memory.
  • LinkedBlockingQueue — linked nodes, optionally bounded (unbounded by default!), separate put/take locks so higher throughput.
  • SynchronousQueue — capacity zero: each put() waits for a matching take(). A direct hand-off, used by Executors.newCachedThreadPool().

Backpressure: when consumers fall behind, a bounded queue fills up and put() blocks — producers are forcibly slowed to the consumers' pace instead of piling up work until the JVM runs out of memory. An unbounded queue provides no such signal; that's the hidden risk of default LinkedBlockingQueue in fixed thread pools.

questions.question: How does thread interruption work in Java? What does `interrupt()` actually do, what is the difference between `isInterrupted()` and `Thread.interrupted()`, and how should a well-behaved task respond to an `InterruptedException`?
questions.explanation:
Interruption is cooperative: t.interrupt() doesn't stop the thread. It sets the thread's interrupted flag, and if the thread is blocked in sleep(), wait(), join() or a blocking queue op, that method wakes up, clears the flag, and throws InterruptedException. Running code must check the flag itself.

  • isInterrupted() — instance method, reads the flag, leaves it set.
  • Thread.interrupted() — static, reads the flag of the current thread and clears it. Calling it twice returns false the second time.
try {
    queue.take();
} catch (InterruptedException e) {
    Thread.currentThread().interrupt(); // restore the flag
    return; // stop the task promptly
}

A well-behaved task either propagates the exception, or catches it, does cleanup, re-sets the flag and exits. The one thing you must never do is swallow it with an empty catch — that erases the cancellation signal and the pool/caller can no longer stop the work.

questions.question: What is `ThreadLocal`, how is it implemented, and where is it used in frameworks (transactions, security context, MDC)? Explain why `ThreadLocal` leaks memory in thread pools if not removed, and why it interacts badly with async code.
questions.explanation:
ThreadLocal<T> gives each thread its own independent copy of a variable — a way to pass per-request state without threading it through every method signature.

static final ThreadLocal<User> CURRENT = new ThreadLocal<>();
CURRENT.set(user);      // visible only to this thread
...
User u = CURRENT.get(); // anywhere down the call stack
CURRENT.remove();       // in a finally block!

Implementation: each Thread owns a ThreadLocalMap keyed by the ThreadLocal object (held via a weak reference), so lookup is on the current thread's own map — no locking needed. Framework uses: Spring transaction context, SecurityContextHolder, SLF4J MDC (log correlation ids).

Leak in pools: pool threads never die, so their maps live forever. The weak key can be collected but the value stays until the slot is overwritten — and worse, the next request served by that thread sees the previous request's stale data. Hence remove() in finally. Async problem: a CompletableFuture stage may run on a different thread than the one that called set(), so the value silently isn't there — context must be captured and restored explicitly (or use ScopedValue / context-propagation libraries).

questions.question: What is a read-write lock (`ReentrantReadWriteLock`) and when does it actually help? Explain reader starvation vs writer starvation, and what `StampedLock` adds with optimistic reads.
questions.explanation:
ReentrantReadWriteLock splits one lock into a shared read lock (many readers concurrently) and an exclusive write lock (one writer, no readers). It pays off only when reads are frequent, long enough to matter, and writes are rare — for short critical sections its bookkeeping makes it slower than a plain ReentrantLock or synchronized.

rw.readLock().lock();  // N threads may hold this together
rw.writeLock().lock(); // exclusive; waits for all readers

Starvation: in non-fair mode, with a steady stream of readers the read count may never drop to zero, so a waiting writer starves (the common case; the JDK mitigates it by blocking new readers once a writer queues). In fair mode strict FIFO can instead make readers wait behind writers, hurting read throughput.

StampedLock adds optimistic reading: tryOptimisticRead() takes no lock at all, just a stamp; you read your fields, then validate(stamp) — if a write happened meanwhile, you retry or upgrade to a real read lock. Great for read-mostly data since optimistic readers cost nearly nothing — but it's not reentrant and throws no InterruptedException by default, so use it carefully.

questions.question: You have a cache accessed by many threads where computing a missing value is expensive. Compare implementations: `synchronized` map with get-then-put, `ConcurrentHashMap.computeIfAbsent`, and per-key `Future`-based memoization. What race conditions and pitfalls does each address (including recursive `computeIfAbsent`)?
questions.explanation:
The problem: many threads ask for the same missing key at once — you want the expensive computation to run exactly once while others wait for its result.

  • synchronized map, get-then-put: check-then-act race. Two threads both see null, both compute, one result overwrites the other — duplicate work (holding the lock during compute fixes the race but serializes all cache access).
  • ConcurrentHashMap.computeIfAbsent: atomic per key — the mapping function runs once, other threads asking for that key block until it finishes. Pitfalls: it locks the hash bin during the computation, so a long compute stalls unrelated keys in the same bin; and a mapping function that re-enters computeIfAbsent on the same map (recursive fill) can throw IllegalStateException or deadlock.
  • Per-key Future memoization (Goetz's Memoizer): store a FutureTask in the map, not the value.
var ft = new FutureTask<>(() -> expensiveCompute(key));
var prev = cache.putIfAbsent(key, ft); // atomic claim
if (prev == null) { ft.run(); prev = ft; }
return prev.get(); // everyone waits on the same computation

This computes once, doesn't hold any map lock during the computation, and waiting threads block only on their key. Remaining care: remove the entry on failure so a thrown exception isn't cached forever.

questions.question: Explain the lifecycle of a Java thread (NEW, RUNNABLE, BLOCKED, WAITING, TIMED_WAITING, TERMINATED). What is the difference between BLOCKED and WAITING, and which operations put a thread into each state?
questions.explanation:
A thread moves through: NEW (created, not started) → RUNNABLE (running or ready to run) → possibly BLOCKED / WAITING / TIMED_WAITING → TERMINATED (run() finished).

The exam-favorite distinction:

  • BLOCKED — the thread wants to enter a synchronized block but another thread holds the monitor. It is stuck on lock acquisition; the JVM will hand it the lock when free.
  • WAITING — the thread chose to park until another thread signals it: wait(), join(), LockSupport.park(). Nothing wakes it except an explicit notify()/completion/unpark.
  • TIMED_WAITING — same, but with a deadline: sleep(ms), wait(ms), join(ms).
synchronized (lock) {            // another thread inside → you are BLOCKED
    while (!ready) lock.wait();  // you gave up the monitor → WAITING
}
Thread.sleep(1000);              // TIMED_WAITING

Takeaway: BLOCKED = fighting for a lock; WAITING = deliberately parked until signalled.

questions.question: What is a deadlock? Describe the four conditions required for one, show a minimal two-lock example, and explain how you would detect a deadlock in a running production JVM and how lock ordering prevents it.
questions.explanation:
A deadlock needs all four Coffman conditions: mutual exclusion, hold-and-wait, no preemption, and circular wait. Break any one and deadlock is impossible.

// Thread 1                      // Thread 2
synchronized (a) {               synchronized (b) {
    synchronized (b) { ... }         synchronized (a) { ... }
}                                }
// T1 holds a, wants b; T2 holds b, wants a → cycle → deadlock
  • Detect in production: jstack <pid> (or jcmd <pid> Thread.print) — the thread dump ends with "Found one Java-level deadlock" and shows exactly which threads hold and want which monitors.
  • Prevent: a global lock order — every thread takes a before b. Then no cycle can ever form, because circular wait is structurally impossible.

questions.question: How does the low-level `wait()`/`notify()`/`notifyAll()` mechanism work? Why must they be called while holding the monitor, why should `wait()` always sit in a loop checking a condition (spurious wakeups), and what higher-level tools replaced this pattern?
questions.explanation:
wait()/notify() let threads coordinate through an object's monitor: wait() releases the monitor and parks the thread; notify()/notifyAll() wake waiter(s), who then re-acquire the monitor and continue. All three throw IllegalMonitorStateException unless you hold the monitor — the JVM requires this so that checking the condition and going to sleep is atomic (otherwise a notify could slip in between and be lost).

synchronized (queue) {
    while (queue.isEmpty()) {   // WHILE, never if
        queue.wait();           // releases the monitor while parked
    }
    item = queue.remove();
}
// producer:
synchronized (queue) { queue.add(x); queue.notifyAll(); }

The loop is mandatory because of spurious wakeups (a thread may wake with no notify) and because another thread may have consumed the condition first — so re-check after every wakeup. In modern code you use the higher-level replacements: BlockingQueue, CountDownLatch, Semaphore, CompletableFuture, or Lock+Condition.

questions.question: Explain `ExecutorService` thread pools: what do core pool size, max pool size, the work queue and the rejection policy each control? Walk through the (surprising) order in which a `ThreadPoolExecutor` uses them when tasks arrive.
questions.explanation:
A ThreadPoolExecutor has four knobs: core pool size (threads kept alive even when idle), max pool size (hard ceiling), a work queue (where tasks wait), and a rejection policy (what to do when everything is full).

The surprising part is the order when a task arrives:

  1. Fewer than core threads? → create a new thread, even if others are idle.
  2. Core full? → queue the task — it does NOT grow the pool yet.
  3. Queue full too? → only now create extra threads up to max.
  4. At max and queue full? → apply the rejection policy (default AbortPolicy throws RejectedExecutionException).
new ThreadPoolExecutor(2, 4, 60, SECONDS,
        new LinkedBlockingQueue<>(100));   // grows to 3rd thread only after 100 tasks queue up

Takeaway: with an unbounded queue (like Executors.newFixedThreadPool) the max pool size is never reached — step 3 requires the queue to fill first.

questions.question: How would you size a thread pool for CPU-bound work vs I/O-bound work? Explain the reasoning (Little's law intuition is fine), and what goes wrong when blocking I/O tasks run on a small shared pool like the common ForkJoinPool.
questions.explanation:
CPU-bound: threads compete for cores, so more threads than cores just adds context-switching. Size ≈ cores (or cores + 1). I/O-bound: threads spend most time waiting, so you need extra threads to keep cores busy: threads ≈ cores × (1 + waitTime / computeTime). That's the Little's-law intuition — throughput = concurrency / latency, so high wait time demands high concurrency.

// 8 cores, task waits 90ms on I/O per 10ms of CPU:
// 8 * (1 + 90/10) = 80 threads to saturate the CPUs

What goes wrong on a small shared pool: the common ForkJoinPool has ~cores threads and is shared by parallel streams and default CompletableFuture stages. If tasks block on I/O there, all workers sit parked, nothing else (including unrelated parallel streams anywhere in the JVM) can run — effective deadlock/starvation. Takeaway: blocking work belongs on its own bounded executor, never the common pool.

questions.question: Compare `Runnable`, `Callable` and `Future`. What happens to an exception thrown inside a task submitted with `submit()` vs `execute()`, and why do thread pools sometimes 'swallow' exceptions silently?
questions.explanation:
Runnable = task with no result, can't throw checked exceptions. Callable<V> = returns a value and may throw. Future<V> = handle to the pending result: get() blocks, cancel() attempts interruption.

Future<Integer> f = pool.submit(() -> 1 / 0);
// nothing visible yet...
f.get(); // throws ExecutionException wrapping ArithmeticException

Exception paths: with execute(Runnable) the exception escapes the task, reaches the thread's UncaughtExceptionHandler and is printed (the worker thread may die and be replaced). With submit() the pool catches the exception and stores it inside the Future — it only resurfaces as an ExecutionException when someone calls get().

Why exceptions get 'swallowed': fire-and-forget submit() where nobody ever calls get() — the exception sits in the discarded Future forever. Takeaway: either get() every Future, or use execute() / a wrapper that logs.

questions.question: What is compare-and-swap (CAS) and how do atomic classes use it to avoid locks? Explain the retry loop inside `AtomicInteger.incrementAndGet()`, the ABA problem, and when `LongAdder` beats `AtomicLong`.
questions.explanation:
CAS is an atomic CPU instruction: "if the value at this address is still expected, replace it with new, else fail". No lock, no blocking — a failed CAS just means someone else won the race and you retry.

// essence of AtomicInteger.incrementAndGet()
int prev, next;
do {
    prev = value.get();      // read current
    next = prev + 1;         // compute
} while (!value.compareAndSet(prev, next)); // retry if changed

ABA problem: CAS only compares values. If the value went A → B → A while you weren't looking, CAS succeeds even though state changed — dangerous for lock-free stacks reusing nodes. Fix: AtomicStampedReference pairs the value with a version stamp.

LongAdder vs AtomicLong: under heavy contention every thread CAS-retries on one variable and most fail. LongAdder stripes the count across multiple cells (threads hit different cells) and sums them on read. Use it for hot write-mostly counters; use AtomicLong when you need exact reads or CAS semantics.

questions.question: Describe the synchronizers in `java.util.concurrent`: `CountDownLatch`, `CyclicBarrier`, `Semaphore` and `Phaser`. Give a concrete use case for each and explain which of them are reusable.
questions.explanation:
All four coordinate threads around a "wait until…" condition, differing in who waits and reusability.

  • CountDownLatch — one-shot gate. Threads await() until the count hits 0 via countDown(). Use: main thread waits for N services to finish startup. Not reusable.
  • CyclicBarrier — N parties wait for each other at a common point, then all proceed (optionally running a barrier action). Use: iterative simulation where all workers must finish step k before step k+1. Reusable (resets each cycle).
  • Semaphore — a pool of permits; acquire() blocks when none left, release() returns one. Use: cap concurrent access to a resource, e.g. at most 10 simultaneous DB connections. Reusable.
  • Phaser — like a reusable barrier but parties can register/deregister dynamically and it tracks phase numbers. Use: multi-stage pipeline where the number of workers changes between stages. Reusable.

Takeaway: latch = wait for events (one-shot); barrier/phaser = threads wait for each other (cyclic); semaphore = limit concurrency.

questions.question: In the Stream API, what does it mean that intermediate operations are lazy? Explain how a stream pipeline actually executes, and give a case where a parallel stream makes performance worse.
questions.explanation:
Intermediate operations (filter, map, peek…) do nothing when called — they just build a recipe. Only a terminal operation (forEach, toList, findFirst…) starts execution, and then elements flow through the whole pipeline one at a time, not stage by stage.

Stream.of("a", "bb", "ccc")
    .filter(s -> { System.out.println("filter " + s); return s.length() > 1; })
    .map(s -> { System.out.println("map " + s); return s.toUpperCase(); })
    .findFirst();
// prints: filter a, filter bb, map bb — then stops.
// "ccc" is never touched; nothing runs before findFirst() is called.

Takeaway: each element is pulled through all stages before the next element starts, and short-circuiting terminals (findFirst, anyMatch, limit) stop pulling as soon as they have an answer — that is what laziness buys you.

Parallel streams split the source into chunks, process them on the shared ForkJoinPool and merge the results. That overhead makes them slower when the collection is small, the per-element work is trivial, the pipeline is ordered or stateful (sorted, limit), or the merge is expensive (e.g. collecting into a map).

questions.question: How does `ConcurrentHashMap` provide thread safety without locking the whole map? Why is a get-then-put sequence still a race condition on it, and which methods solve that atomically?
questions.explanation:
Intuition: instead of one big lock, ConcurrentHashMap locks at the level of a single hash bin. Reads are lock-free (the table and nodes use volatile semantics), and writes either CAS an empty bin or synchronized only on the first node of that one bin — so threads touching different keys almost never contend.

But that only makes each call atomic. A get-then-put is two calls, and another thread can slip in between:

// RACE: two threads both see null, both put — one increment is lost
Integer v = map.get("hits");
map.put("hits", v == null ? 1 : v + 1);

// ATOMIC: the whole read-modify-write runs under the bin's lock
map.merge("hits", 1, Integer::sum);
map.computeIfAbsent("users", k -> loadUser(k));
map.putIfAbsent("config", defaults);

Takeaway: thread-safe methods don't compose into thread-safe sequences — for check-then-act use putIfAbsent, computeIfAbsent, compute, merge, or replace(key, old, new).

questions.question: Compare `CompletableFuture` composition with virtual threads (Project Loom): how does each deal with blocking I/O, and how do virtual threads change the argument for writing async, callback-style code?
questions.explanation:
The problem both solve: a platform (OS) thread is expensive (~1 MB stack, kernel resources), so you can't afford 10,000 of them each blocked on a slow HTTP call.

CompletableFuture answers by not blocking: you break the logic into callbacks and a small thread pool hops between them.

client.getUserAsync(id)                 // returns CompletableFuture<User>
      .thenCompose(u -> client.getOrdersAsync(u))
      .thenApply(this::toDto)
      .exceptionally(this::fallback);

The cost: control flow is inverted — stack traces, debugging, loops and try/catch all get harder, and one accidental blocking call inside a stage can starve the pool.

Virtual threads make blocking cheap instead of avoiding it. A virtual thread is a tiny JVM-managed stack mounted on a carrier thread; when it hits blocking I/O the JVM unmounts it and the carrier runs another virtual thread. So this scales to millions:

try (var exec = Executors.newVirtualThreadPerTaskExecutor()) {
    exec.submit(() -> {
        User u = client.getUser(id);      // blocks — but only the virtual thread
        return toDto(client.getOrders(u)); // plain sequential code
    });
}

Takeaway: callback-style async existed mainly to save scarce threads. With Loom that reason disappears — write straight-line blocking code per task, and keep CompletableFuture for what it's still best at: fan-out/fan-in composition (e.g. allOf over parallel calls), not scalability.

questions.question: Java has a garbage collector, yet production services still leak memory. Name typical leak sources (e.g. static collections, listeners, `ThreadLocal` in thread pools, caches) and describe how you would confirm and locate a leak in a running JVM.
questions.explanation:
Intuition: the GC only frees objects that are unreachable. A 'leak' in Java is a reachability bug — something you no longer need is still referenced from a GC root.

  • Static collections — a static Map cache that only ever grows; reachable for the lifetime of the class.
  • Listeners — registered with a long-lived subject and never unregistered; the subject pins every listener (and everything it references).
  • ThreadLocal in pools — pool threads live forever, so each thread's ThreadLocal map keeps the value unless you call remove().
  • Caches without bounds — no max size, no TTL, no weak/soft references.
// leak: pool thread survives the request, value stays reachable
private static final ThreadLocal<byte[]> BUF =
        ThreadLocal.withInitial(() -> new byte[1_000_000]);
// fix: try { use(BUF.get()); } finally { BUF.remove(); }

Confirm & locate: (1) watch heap after full GCs — a rising floor means a leak; (2) jcmd <pid> GC.heap_dump dump.hprof; (3) open it in Eclipse MAT and look at the dominator tree: the leaking structure shows up as a huge retained size, and 'path to GC roots' tells you exactly who is holding it.

questions.question: Is Java pass-by-value or pass-by-reference? Explain what is actually copied when you pass an object to a method, and why a classic `swap(a, b)` method cannot work in Java while mutating a passed object's fields does.
questions.explanation:
Intuition: every argument in Java is copied. For primitives you copy the number; for objects you copy the reference (a pointer-sized value), never the object itself. Both copies point at the same heap object.

void swap(Point a, Point b) { Point t = a; a = b; b = t; }
// caller: swap(p1, p2)  -> p1, p2 UNCHANGED
// only the method's local copies were exchanged

void move(Point a) { a.x = 42; }
// caller: move(p1)      -> p1.x IS 42 now
// the copy still points at the caller's object, so mutation is visible

Takeaway: assignment to a parameter rebinds only the local copy; a method call through the parameter dereferences the shared object. That's the whole pass-by-value story — C#-style ref/out parameters simply don't exist in Java.

questions.question: Why is `String` immutable in Java, and what is the string pool? What is the difference between `"abc"` and `new String("abc")`?
questions.explanation:
Strings are shared all over a JVM, so they must be immutable — and immutability is exactly what makes the string pool safe: the JVM keeps one shared copy of every string literal, and every use of "abc" anywhere in the program points at that same object.

String a = "abc";              // pooled instance
String b = "abc";              // SAME pooled instance
String c = new String("abc");  // forces a NEW heap object

a == b            // true  – both point at the pool
a == c            // false – different objects
a.equals(c)       // true  – same characters
c.intern() == a   // true  – intern() returns the pooled twin

Why immutability matters: String caches its hashCode, so a mutable string used as a HashMap key would change its hash and become unfindable; and a value validated once (a file path, an SQL fragment, a class name) can never be swapped out afterwards — that is the security argument.

Takeaway: never write new String("literal"), and always compare strings with equals(), never ==.

questions.question: What is the relationship between JPA and Hibernate? Explain the roles of EntityManager, the persistence context and the first-level cache.
questions.explanation:
JPA is only a specification — a set of interfaces and annotations (EntityManager, @Entity, JPQL). Hibernate is the most popular implementation: it does the real work of generating SQL, tracking changes and caching.

  Your code
     │  calls JPA interfaces (jakarta.persistence)
     ▼
  EntityManager ── owns ──▶ Persistence Context  (= 1st-level cache)
     │                       [ id → managed entity + snapshot ]
     ▼
  Hibernate Session → JDBC → Database

The EntityManager is your gateway for one unit of work: persist(), find(), merge(), remove(). Everything it loads or persists becomes managed inside its persistence context — and that context is exactly what people call the first-level cache:

User a = em.find(User.class, 1L); // SELECT ... FROM users WHERE id=1
User b = em.find(User.class, 1L); // no SQL at all — served from the context
assert a == b;                    // same Java instance, guaranteed
Takeaway: persistence context = first-level cache. It is per-EntityManager (in practice: per transaction), cannot be turned off, and gives you repeatable reads + identity within one transaction. Dirty and persistent describe entity states inside it, not kinds of EntityManagers.
Don't confuse it with the second-level cache: L2 is optional, shared across EntityManagers (e.g. Ehcache/Infinispan), and needs explicit configuration — the first-level cache is neither optional nor shared.

questions.question: Describe the entity lifecycle states in Hibernate (transient, managed/persistent, detached, removed). What is dirty checking and when does Hibernate actually flush changes to the database?
questions.explanation:
An entity is always in one of four lifecycle states, depending on whether the persistence context is tracking it:

  new User()          persist()                    context closes / detach()
  ───────────▶ TRANSIENT ─────▶ MANAGED ────────────────▶ DETACHED
                                  │   ◀───── merge() ─────────┘
                               remove()
                                  ▼
                               REMOVED ── flush/commit ──▶ row deleted
StateIn context?Has DB row?Changes tracked?
Transientnonono
Managedyesyes (or will at flush)yes — dirty checking
Detachedno longeryesno
Removedyes (marked)until flushscheduled for DELETE

Dirty checking: when an entity becomes managed, Hibernate keeps a snapshot of its loaded state. At flush time it compares current values against the snapshot and generates UPDATEs automatically — you never call save() on a managed entity:

em.getTransaction().begin();
User u = em.find(User.class, 1L);  // managed, snapshot taken
u.setEmail("new@example.com");     // just a setter — no repository call
em.getTransaction().commit();      // flush: snapshot differs → UPDATE users SET email=...
When does Hibernate flush?
  • Before transaction commit (the usual case)
  • Before executing a JPQL/SQL query that might touch tables with pending changes (so the query sees your writes)
  • On an explicit em.flush()
Gotcha: flush ≠ commit. Flush sends the SQL; only commit makes it durable. A flushed UPDATE can still be rolled back.

questions.question: What is the N+1 select problem? Show how it arises with lazy-loaded associations and describe at least two ways to fix it (e.g. fetch join, @EntityGraph, batch fetching).
questions.explanation:
The N+1 problem: one query loads N parent rows, then touching a lazy association on each parent fires one extra SELECT per row. No loop, no recursion — just death by N tiny queries.

List<Order> orders = em.createQuery("select o from Order o", Order.class)
                       .getResultList();          // query #1
for (Order o : orders) {
    o.getCustomer().getName();  // lazy proxy hit → query #2, #3, ... #N+1
}
SQL log for N = 3 orders:
  select ... from orders                     -- 1
  select ... from customer where id = 42     -- +1
  select ... from customer where id = 7      -- +1
  select ... from customer where id = 99     -- +1
  → 4 round-trips where 1 would do

Fix 1 — fetch join: pull the association in the same SQL query.

select o from Order o join fetch o.customer

Fix 2 — @EntityGraph: keep the query generic, declare what to fetch per call site.

@EntityGraph(attributePaths = "customer")
List<Order> findAll();   // Spring Data — generates the join for you

Fix 3 — batch fetching: keep it lazy, but load proxies in groups instead of one by one.

@BatchSize(size = 50)              // on the association or entity
// → select ... from customer where id in (?, ?, ..., ?)   -- N/50 queries
Not a fix: switching the mapping to EAGER. That only hides the problem — list queries still N+1 (or degenerate into giant joins) and you lose the ability to opt out per use case.
Takeaway: count the queries in your SQL log. Rule of thumb: mappings LAZY by default, then fetch join / entity graph exactly where a use case needs the data.

questions.question: Compare LAZY and EAGER fetching. Why is EAGER on collections considered harmful, and what causes a LazyInitializationException? Why is the Open Session in View pattern controversial?
questions.explanation:
LAZYEAGER
Loaded whenfirst access (proxy)always, with the owner
Per-query controlyes — fetch join / @EntityGraph when neededno — cannot opt out
RiskLazyInitializationException, N+1overfetching, cartesian joins

Why EAGER collections hurt: every load of the owner pulls the whole collection — for a list of 100 parents that is 100 collections nobody asked for. Two eager collections on one entity join-multiply rows (m×n duplicates) or throw MultipleBagFetchException. And since EAGER is baked into the mapping, no use case can escape it.

LazyInitializationException — touching a lazy proxy after its session is gone:

@Transactional
public Order load(long id) { return repo.findById(id); }  // tx ends here

// later, in the controller — persistence context already closed:
order.getItems().size();   // → LazyInitializationException

The proxy needs a live session to run its SELECT; outside the transaction there is none. Real fixes: fetch the data inside the transaction (fetch join / @EntityGraph) or map to a DTO before returning.

Open Session in View (Spring: spring.jpa.open-in-view, default true) keeps the session open for the entire HTTP request, so lazy loading silently works during view rendering / JSON serialization. Controversial because it hides N+1 queries in the presentation layer, holds a DB connection while the response is being written, and runs those late queries outside your service-level transaction. Most teams disable it and fetch deliberately.

questions.question: How does Hibernate handle concurrent modifications? Compare optimistic locking with @Version against pessimistic locking, and explain what happens when an OptimisticLockException is thrown.
questions.explanation:
Two transactions editing the same row will silently overwrite each other ("lost update") unless you pick a locking strategy. Hibernate offers two:

Optimistic locking — assume conflicts are rare, detect them at write time via a version column:

@Entity class Product {
    @Id Long id;
    @Version int version;   // Hibernate manages it — never set it yourself
    BigDecimal price;
}
TX A: SELECT product (version = 1)
TX B: SELECT product (version = 1)
TX A: UPDATE product SET price=.., version=2 WHERE id=1 AND version=1  → 1 row ✓ commit
TX B: UPDATE product SET price=.., version=2 WHERE id=1 AND version=1  → 0 rows ✗
      └─▶ OptimisticLockException — somebody changed it under you

Pessimistic locking — assume conflicts are likely, block others up front with a DB row lock:

Product p = em.find(Product.class, 1L, LockModeType.PESSIMISTIC_WRITE);
// SELECT ... FOR UPDATE — other writers now wait (or time out) until we commit
Optimistic (@Version)Pessimistic (FOR UPDATE)
Costnone until a conflictlocks + waiting on every access
Conflict shows asOptimisticLockException at flush/commitblocking, lock timeout, deadlock risk
Best forweb apps, rare conflicts, long think-timehot rows, short transactions (inventory counters)
When OptimisticLockException is thrown the transaction is marked rollback-only and the persistence context is unusable — you cannot just "save again". The correct handling is to start a new transaction, re-read the fresh row, re-apply the change (or ask the user), and retry the whole unit of work — e.g. with Spring's @Retryable around the service method.

questions.question: Java is 'compiled to bytecode, then JIT-compiled'. Explain tiered compilation: what do the interpreter, C1 and C2 each contribute, and what is deoptimization — when does the JVM throw compiled code away?
questions.explanation:
The big picture: javac compiles Java to bytecode. At runtime HotSpot executes that bytecode in three escalating modes — interpreter, C1, C2 — promoting each method up the tiers as it proves itself hot. This is tiered compilation.

Tier 0 — the interpreter: executes bytecode instruction-by-instruction. Slow per instruction, but starts instantly and, crucially, collects profiling data: invocation counters, loop back-edge counters, which branches are taken, and which concrete types appear at call sites.

Tiers 1–3 — C1, the client compiler: once a method gets warm (its counters cross a threshold), C1 compiles it to native code fast, with only light optimizations (basic inlining, constant folding). Tier-3 C1 code keeps profiling instrumentation in, so the JVM keeps learning while already running much faster than interpreting.

Tier 4 — C2, the server compiler: for truly hot methods. Slow to compile, but produces heavily optimized code driven by the gathered profile: aggressive inlining, loop unrolling, escape analysis, vectorization — and speculative optimizations that assume the profile stays true ('this branch is never taken', 'only one class ever implements this interface'). Compiled code is stored in the code cache.

Deoptimization: speculative code carries guards called uncommon traps. When an assumption breaks, the trap fires, the JVM throws the compiled code away (marks it 'made not entrant'), transfers the running frame back to the interpreter, re-profiles, and later recompiles under the new reality.

interface Shape { double area(); }   // only Circle is loaded so far

double total(List<Shape> shapes) {
  double sum = 0;
  for (Shape s : shapes)
    sum += s.area(); // C2: monomorphic -> devirtualized + inlined, no virtual call
  return sum;
}

// later: new Square() gets class-loaded
//  -> assumption 'only Circle implements Shape' is now false
//  -> uncommon trap fires -> method DEOPTIMIZED back to the interpreter
//  -> re-profiled -> recompiled with a real virtual dispatch

Key words:

  • OSR (on-stack replacement) — swapping a long-running loop onto compiled code mid-execution, without waiting for the method to be re-entered
  • Code cache (-XX:ReservedCodeCacheSize) — where JIT output lives; if it fills up, compilation stops and performance degrades
  • Monomorphic vs megamorphic call sites — one receiver type (inlinable) vs many (plain virtual dispatch)
  • -XX:+PrintCompilation — watch methods climb the tiers and spot made not entrant on deopt

Takeaway: interpreter = start instantly and gather facts; C1 = quick, decent code; C2 = gamble on the facts for maximum speed; deoptimization = the safety net that makes the gamble safe.

questions.question: What is Micronaut and what problems with traditional JVM frameworks (like classic Spring) was it designed to solve? Name its three headline characteristics.
questions.explanation:
Classic Spring builds its dependency-injection container at runtime: it scans the classpath, reads annotations via reflection, and wires beans while the app boots. That costs startup time and memory — painful for microservices, serverless functions, and containers that scale up/down constantly. Micronaut moves all of that to compile time.

Its three headline characteristics:

  • Fast startup — the DI wiring already exists as generated classes; nothing to scan or reflect on at boot.
  • Low memory footprint — no reflection metadata caches or runtime proxies to hold in memory.
  • Compile-time DI and AOP — annotation processors generate the container during javac, which also makes GraalVM native images (millisecond startup) practical.

Takeaway: Micronaut is 'Spring-like DX, but the framework does its heavy lifting in the compiler, not at boot.'

questions.question: Explain server and client filters (`HttpServerFilter` / `HttpClientFilter`): what are they for, how do you control their order and the paths they apply to, and what are typical use cases?
questions.explanation:
Filters are interceptors around HTTP traffic: HttpServerFilter wraps incoming requests before they reach a controller; HttpClientFilter wraps outgoing requests from HTTP clients. Each gets the request plus a chain to pass control on — letting it act before and after.

@Filter("/api/**")                    // path pattern it applies to
class AuthFilter implements HttpServerFilter {
  public Publisher<MutableHttpResponse<?>> doFilter(
      HttpRequest<?> req, ServerFilterChain chain) {
    if (!hasValidToken(req)) return unauthorized();
    return chain.proceed(req);        // continue to controller
  }
}
  • Paths: the @Filter("/api/**") pattern decides which URLs the filter sees.
  • Order: implement getOrder() or use ServerFilterPhase constants (SECURITY, TRACING...) so e.g. auth runs before metrics.
  • Typical uses: authentication, request logging, metrics/tracing, CORS; on the client side — injecting auth headers or propagating correlation IDs.

Takeaway: cross-cutting HTTP concerns live in filters, not copied into every controller or client.

questions.question: How does error handling work in Micronaut controllers? Compare local and global `@Error` handlers and `ExceptionHandler` beans — when would you use each?
questions.explanation:
Micronaut has three layers for turning an exception into an HTTP response. The lookup order:

exception thrown in a controller method
        |
        v
1. local @Error in the SAME controller?  --yes--> handles it
        | no
        v
2. global @Error (global = true)?        --yes--> handles it
        | no
        v
3. ExceptionHandler<E, R> bean for E?    --yes--> handles it
        | no
        v
   default 500 Internal Server Error

Example 1 — local @Error: response formatting specific to one controller.

@Controller("/orders")
class OrderController {
  @Post
  Order create(@Body OrderRequest r) { ... }

  @Error(exception = OutOfStockException.class)  // only THIS controller
  HttpResponse<JsonError> outOfStock(OutOfStockException e) {
    return HttpResponse.badRequest(new JsonError(e.getMessage()));
  }
}

Example 2 — global @Error: one JSON shape for every 404 in the app.

@Controller
class GlobalErrors {
  @Error(status = HttpStatus.NOT_FOUND, global = true)
  HttpResponse<JsonError> notFound(HttpRequest<?> req) {
    return HttpResponse.notFound(new JsonError("Nothing at " + req.getPath()));
  }
}

Example 3 — ExceptionHandler bean: maps one domain exception app-wide; lives next to the domain code, not in a controller.

@Singleton
class BookNotFoundHandler
    implements ExceptionHandler<BookNotFoundException, HttpResponse<?>> {
  public HttpResponse<?> handle(HttpRequest req, BookNotFoundException e) {
    return HttpResponse.notFound(new JsonError("book " + e.getId() + " not found"));
  }
}

Rule of thumb: local @Error = controller-specific formatting; global @Error = cross-cutting status pages; ExceptionHandler = reusable domain-exception → response mapping (Micronaut itself uses this, e.g. ConstraintViolationException → 400).

questions.question: How does validation work in Micronaut (`@Valid`, `@Validated`, jakarta.validation annotations)? What part of the validation support is done at compile time, and what does that buy you?
questions.explanation:
Micronaut uses the standard jakarta.validation annotations (@NotBlank, @Min, @Email...), but the validator is generated at compile time — no reflection-based Hibernate Validator at runtime. Request flow:

POST /users  {"name": "", "email": "x"}
     |
     v
JSON deserialized into UserRequest
     |
     v
compile-time-generated validator runs the constraints (no reflection)
     |
     +-- violations? --> automatic 400 + messages (method never runs)
     |
     v
controller method executes

Example 1 — validating a request body with @Valid @Body:

@Introspected
record UserRequest(@NotBlank String name, @Email String email) {}

@Controller("/users")
class UserController {
  @Post
  HttpResponse<?> create(@Valid @Body UserRequest r) { ... }  // invalid → 400
}

Example 2 — method parameters on any bean: constraints on a service method make Micronaut weave a validating AOP interceptor around it at compile time (this is what @Validated marked explicitly in older versions):

@Singleton
class MailService {
  void send(@NotBlank String to, @NotBlank String subject) { ... }
  // blank argument → ConstraintViolationException before the body runs
}

Example 3 — configuration validation: constraints on @ConfigurationProperties fail at startup with a clear message instead of misbehaving later:

@ConfigurationProperties("app.mail")
class MailConfig {
  @NotBlank String host;
  @Min(1) int port;
}

What compile time buys you: the annotation processor generates reflection-free validation code and bean-introspection metadata — fast startup, GraalVM native-image compatibility, and misplaced constraints are reported by the compiler instead of blowing up in production.

questions.question: Explain AOP in Micronaut: how do you write a `MethodInterceptor` with an `@Around` annotation, and how do Micronaut's compile-time proxies differ from Spring's runtime JDK/CGLIB proxies (including the self-invocation problem)?
questions.explanation:
Micronaut AOP = you define an annotation marked @Around, plus a MethodInterceptor that runs around every method carrying that annotation.

Example 1 — a @Timed annotation and its interceptor:

@Retention(RUNTIME)
@Target({METHOD, TYPE})
@Around                       // marks it as around-advice
@interface Timed {}

@Singleton
@InterceptorBean(Timed.class)
class TimedInterceptor implements MethodInterceptor<Object, Object> {
  public Object intercept(MethodInvocationContext<Object, Object> ctx) {
    long t0 = System.nanoTime();
    try { return ctx.proceed(); }               // call the real method
    finally { System.out.println(ctx.getMethodName() + " took " + (System.nanoTime() - t0) + " ns"); }
  }
}

Example 2 — how the call flows. The proxy is generated during compilation as a subclass, and that subclass IS the bean in the context:

Micronaut (compile time):
 caller --> $ReportService$Intercepted (generated subclass = the bean)
              --> TimedInterceptor.intercept()
                    --> ctx.proceed() --> original method body

Spring (runtime):
 caller --> proxy (JDK dynamic proxy / CGLIB, built at startup)
              --> advice --> target.method()   // proxy WRAPS a separate raw instance

Example 3 — the self-invocation difference:

class ReportService {
  void nightly() { this.compute(); }   // internal call
  @Timed void compute() { ... }
}
  • Spring: inside nightly(), this is the raw target, not the proxy — the advice on compute() is silently skipped (the classic @Transactional self-invocation bug).
  • Micronaut: the bean instance is the generated subclass, so this.compute() dispatches to the overridden, intercepted method — the advice still runs.

Takeaway: compile-time proxies mean no runtime proxy generation (faster startup, native-image friendly) and no self-invocation trap.

questions.question: What is Micronaut Data and how does it differ fundamentally from Spring Data? Explain what happens to a repository interface method like `findByNameAndAgeGreaterThan` at compile time, and why that helps both performance and correctness.
questions.explanation:
Micronaut Data gives you Spring-Data-style repository interfaces, but the query derivation happens in the compiler, not at runtime:

Spring Data (runtime):
  findByNameAndAgeGreaterThan --(reflection + parsing at bootstrap/first use)--> query object --> SQL

Micronaut Data (compile time):
  findByNameAndAgeGreaterThan --(annotation processor during javac)--> final SQL baked
  into a generated implementation class; runtime just executes a prepared statement

Example 1 — a repository and what the compiler makes of it:

@JdbcRepository(dialect = Dialect.POSTGRES)
interface PersonRepository extends CrudRepository<Person, Long> {
  List<Person> findByNameAndAgeGreaterThan(String name, int age);
}
// generated at compile time (roughly):
// SELECT id, name, age FROM person WHERE name = ? AND age > ?

Example 2 — a typo fails the BUILD, not the deploy:

List<Person> findByNmae(String name);
// compile error: Cannot find property 'nmae' in entity Person
// (Spring Data would throw at application startup instead)

Example 3 — paging works the same way, still computed at compile time:

Page<Person> findByAgeGreaterThan(int age, Pageable pageable);

Why it helps: performance — no runtime query parsing, no reflection-based entity mapping (compile-time bean introspection instead), tiny footprint and native-image support; correctness — wrong property names or parameter types are compiler errors caught in CI, not runtime surprises.

questions.question: Micronaut performs dependency injection at compile time using annotation processors. Explain what that means concretely — what is generated during compilation, and what does the framework NOT have to do at runtime compared to a reflection-based container?
questions.explanation:
In Micronaut, an annotation processor runs inside javac. For every bean it sees, it writes real Java classes next to yours:

@Singleton
public class EmailService {
  public EmailService(SmtpClient client) { ... }
}
// compiler additionally generates (simplified):
//   $EmailService$Definition  — knows how to construct the bean
//   introspection metadata    — property/annotation info, no reflection

The generated BeanDefinition contains the injection logic as ordinary compiled code: 'call the constructor with the SmtpClient bean'.

At runtime the framework therefore does not need to: scan the classpath, read annotations via reflection, or build dynamic proxies. It just loads the pre-generated definitions — hence fast startup, low memory, and compile-time errors when wiring is wrong (a missing bean fails the build, not the deploy).

questions.question: Walk through the main bean scopes in Micronaut: `@Singleton`, `@Prototype`, `@RequestScope` and `@Context`. When is a `@Singleton` bean actually created, and how does `@Context` change that?
questions.explanation:
Micronaut bean scopes control how many instances exist and when they're created:

  • @Singleton — one shared instance, created lazily on first request/injection (not at startup!). Default choice for services.
  • @Context — one instance, but created eagerly when the ApplicationContext starts. Use for beans that must validate config or fail fast at boot (e.g. a connection pool).
  • @Prototype — a brand-new instance for every injection point.
  • @RequestScope — one instance per HTTP request, discarded when the request ends.
@Singleton  // built on first use
class PriceCalculator { ... }

@Context    // built at startup, boot fails if constructor throws
class DbMigrationRunner { ... }

Takeaway: Micronaut singletons are lazy by default (unlike Spring); @Context is the opt-in for eager creation.

questions.question: How do you define beans for classes you don't own (e.g. from a third-party library)? Explain `@Factory` classes and `@Bean` methods, and how injection points (constructor vs field) work with `@Inject`.
questions.explanation:
You can't put @Singleton on a class from a third-party JAR — you don't own its source. Instead you declare it in a @Factory class: a bean whose @Bean-annotated methods produce instances, and the annotation processor generates bean definitions for the return values.

@Factory
class ClientsFactory {
  @Bean
  @Singleton   // scope of the produced bean
  ObjectMapper objectMapper() {
    return new ObjectMapper().findAndRegisterModules();
  }
}

Injection points:

  • Constructor injection — the preferred style; a single constructor needs no annotation at all, Micronaut injects its parameters automatically.
  • Field / setter injection — mark with @Inject; works, but fields can't be final and the dependency is hidden from tests.

Takeaway: own classes → annotate the class; foreign classes → build them in a @Factory method; prefer constructor injection everywhere.

questions.question: What does the `@Requires` annotation do? Give three different kinds of conditions it supports (e.g. property, class on classpath, environment) and explain how conditional beans enable Micronaut's modular configuration.
questions.explanation:
@Requires makes a bean conditional: the compile-time-generated bean definition is only activated when its conditions hold at startup. Common condition kinds:

  • Property — @Requires(property = "redis.enabled", value = "true"): bean exists only when configuration says so.
  • Class on classpath — @Requires(classes = RedisClient.class): bean activates only if the library is present.
  • Environment — @Requires(env = "dev") or notEnv: dev-only stubs, cloud-only integrations.
  • Also: beans = DataSource.class (another bean exists), missingBeans = MyService.class (act as a default the user can override).
@Singleton
@Requires(classes = RedisClient.class)
@Requires(property = "cache.type", value = "redis")
class RedisCache implements Cache { ... }

Takeaway: this is how Micronaut modules self-configure — drop a dependency on the classpath, set a property, and the right beans switch on; remove it and they vanish, no central config needed.

questions.question: Compare the ways to consume configuration in Micronaut: `@Value`, `@Property` and `@ConfigurationProperties` classes. When would you choose type-safe configuration binding, and how does it get validated?
questions.explanation:
Three ways to read configuration, from quick-and-dirty to type-safe:

  • @Value("${app.timeout:30}") — placeholder expression with optional default; string-based, typos surface only when the bean is created.
  • @Property(name = "app.timeout") — same idea without placeholder syntax; slightly cleaner for single values.
  • @ConfigurationProperties("app.mail") — binds a whole prefix onto a typed bean: fields host, port map to app.mail.host, app.mail.port.
@ConfigurationProperties("app.mail")
class MailConfig {
  @NotBlank String host;
  @Min(1) int port = 25;
}

Choose type-safe binding when several related values belong together: you get IDE completion, defaults in code, one injectable config object — and validation: with jakarta.validation annotations (@NotBlank, @Min...) plus the validation module, the app fails at startup with a clear message instead of misbehaving later.

questions.question: How do environments work in Micronaut (e.g. `dev`, `test`, `cloud`)? How are environment-specific configuration files resolved, and how does `@Requires(env = ...)` interact with them?
questions.explanation:
An environment is a set of active labels on the ApplicationContext (like Spring profiles). They come from: the micronaut.environments system property or MICRONAUT_ENVIRONMENTS env var, explicit code (Micronaut.build().environments("dev")), and deduction — Micronaut auto-adds test under JUnit, or k8s/ec2 when it detects the platform.

Configuration resolution: application.yml loads first, then application-{env}.yml for each active environment overlays it (later wins). So application-dev.yml only overrides the keys it declares.

application.yml        # shared defaults
application-dev.yml    # local DB url, verbose logging
application-cloud.yml  # real endpoints

@Requires(env = "dev") ties beans to the same mechanism: the bean activates only when that environment label is active — e.g. a fake payment gateway in dev, the real one with @Requires(notEnv = "dev"). Files control values, @Requires(env) controls which beans exist.

questions.question: Describe how an HTTP endpoint is built in Micronaut: `@Controller`, method annotations like `@Get`/`@Post`, and argument binding (path variables, query params, request body). How does routing differ from Spring MVC's runtime handler mapping?
questions.explanation:
An endpoint is a class annotated @Controller with methods annotated @Get, @Post, etc. Method arguments are bound automatically:

@Controller("/books")
class BookController {
  @Get("/{id}")                      // path variable → parameter by name
  Book find(Long id) { ... }

  @Get("/search")                    // ?title=... query param
  List<Book> search(@QueryValue String title) { ... }

  @Post                              // JSON body → object
  HttpResponse<Book> create(@Body Book book) { ... }
}
  • Path variables bind by matching parameter name; @QueryValue binds query params; @Body deserializes the request body; @Header binds headers.

The routing difference: Spring MVC builds its handler mappings at runtime by reflecting over controllers during startup. Micronaut's annotation processor computes the routing table at compile time — routes are generated code, so startup does no controller scanning and argument binding needs no reflection.

questions.question: What is a declarative HTTP client (`@Client` on an interface)? Explain what Micronaut generates for it, how it integrates with service discovery via service IDs, and why this beats hand-writing HTTP calls.
questions.explanation:
A declarative client is an interface you annotate with @Client; Micronaut's annotation processor generates the implementation at compile time — every method becomes a real HTTP call with serialization, URI building and error handling done for you.

@Client(id = "inventory")            // service ID, not a hardcoded URL
interface InventoryClient {
  @Get("/stock/{sku}")
  StockLevel stock(String sku);
}
// inject and call like a local service:
// int n = inventoryClient.stock("ABC-1").getUnits();

With id = "inventory" the target is a service ID resolved through service discovery (Consul, Eureka, Kubernetes) or a static micronaut.http.services.inventory.url property — so code never hardcodes hosts, and load balancing across instances comes free.

Versus hand-written HTTP calls: no boilerplate to maintain, the compiler checks the contract, and the client automatically participates in retries, tracing and metrics. Takeaway: you declare WHAT the remote API looks like; Micronaut generates HOW to call it.

questions.question: Records generate a lot for you — what exactly? Explain the canonical constructor vs a compact constructor, and show how you enforce an invariant (e.g. a non-negative amount) in a record.
questions.explanation:
Canonical constructor = the constructor matching the record header exactly, one parameter per component. It's generated for you, but you can write it out yourself when you need logic. Compact constructor = the same thing with the parameter list and field assignments omitted — you write only the validation/normalization, and the compiler appends this.x = x; for every component at the end.

record Payment(String id, BigDecimal amount) {
    // compact form: no parameter list, no explicit field assignment
    Payment {
        Objects.requireNonNull(id);
        if (amount.signum() < 0)
            throw new IllegalArgumentException("amount must be >= 0");
        amount = amount.setScale(2, RoundingMode.HALF_UP); // reassigns the PARAMETER
    } // compiler adds: this.id = id; this.amount = amount;
}

Takeaway: put invariants in the compact constructor — every way of creating the record (including deserialization via the canonical constructor) goes through it, so an invalid Payment can never exist.

  • Generated members: id(), amount() accessors (not getId()), equals, hashCode, toString, canonical constructor.
  • No setters — fields are final; to "change" a value you build a new record.

questions.question: Explain pattern matching for `instanceof` and record deconstruction patterns in `switch`. How do nested patterns and guards (`when` clauses) let you replace an if-else chain over a data hierarchy?
questions.explanation:
Pattern matching for instanceof tests the type and binds a ready-to-use variable in one step:

// old                                  // new
if (o instanceof String) {              if (o instanceof String s) {
    String s = (String) o;                  use(s.length());
    use(s.length());                    }
}

Record deconstruction in switch goes further: it pulls the components out of a record, can nest, and when adds a boolean guard to a case:

sealed interface Shape permits Circle, Rect {}
record Circle(double r) implements Shape {}
record Rect(Point a, Point b) implements Shape {}
record Point(double x, double y) {}

double area(Shape s) {
    return switch (s) {
        case Circle c when c.r() == 0 -> 0;          // guard
        case Circle(double r)         -> Math.PI * r * r;
        case Rect(Point(var x1, var y1), Point(var x2, var y2))  // nested
                                      -> Math.abs(x2 - x1) * Math.abs(y2 - y1);
    };
}

Takeaway: this replaces an if-else chain of instanceof + cast + getter calls with one declarative switch — and because Shape is sealed, the compiler proves every subtype is handled, with no default needed.

questions.question: Why must you never use `double` or `float` to represent money? Explain how binary floating point stores values, why `0.1 + 0.2 != 0.3`, and show a concrete financial calculation where the error becomes visible.
questions.explanation:
Intuition: binary floating point can only store sums of powers of 2 (1/2, 1/4, 1/8, ...). Decimal 0.1 is 1/10 — and 10 has the prime factor 5, which no power of 2 can produce. So 0.1 in binary is an infinite repeating fraction (0.000110011001100...), and the 52-bit mantissa of a double has to cut it off. The stored value is already wrong before you do any math.

double d = 0.1 + 0.2;
System.out.println(d);                    // 0.30000000000000004
System.out.println(new BigDecimal(0.1));  // 0.1000000000000000055511151231257827...

// financial example: sum a $0.10 fee a million times
double total = 0;
for (int i = 0; i < 1_000_000; i++) total += 0.10;
System.out.println(total);                // 100000.00000133288 — off by real money

Takeaway: the error is in the representation, not the arithmetic — so no amount of careful coding fixes it. For money use BigDecimal (created from a String) or a long count of the smallest unit (cents).

questions.question: What is the difference between `new BigDecimal(0.1)`, `new BigDecimal("0.1")` and `BigDecimal.valueOf(0.1)`? Explain exactly what value each produces and which construction rule you should follow in financial code.
questions.explanation:
Intuition: the damage from binary floating point happens the moment a literal like 0.1 becomes a double. What matters is whether the double's full garbage reaches your BigDecimal, or whether you bypass the double entirely.

new BigDecimal(0.1)      // 0.1000000000000000055511151231257827021181583404541015625
                         // takes the double's EXACT binary value — garbage preserved

new BigDecimal("0.1")    // 0.1  — parses the decimal text, exact, scale 1

BigDecimal.valueOf(0.1)  // 0.1  — shorthand for new BigDecimal(Double.toString(0.1));
                         // toString gives the shortest decimal that round-trips,
                         // so it usually 'repairs' the value

Rule for financial code: always construct from a String (or use BigDecimal.valueOf(long, scale) for integer amounts). valueOf(double) is only a rescue when a double already exists in your data — the correct fix is to never let money become a double in the first place. The new BigDecimal(double) constructor should be treated as forbidden.

questions.question: How would you design a `Money` value type for a trading system? Discuss pairing amount with currency, preventing operations across currencies, choosing and fixing the scale, and the alternative of storing amounts as `long` minor units (cents) — when is each approach better?
questions.explanation:
Pair the amount with its currency in one immutable value type, so a "naked" number can never be mistaken for money and cross-currency math fails fast:

public record Money(BigDecimal amount, Currency currency) {
    public Money {
        amount = amount.setScale(currency.getDefaultFractionDigits(), RoundingMode.UNNECESSARY);
    }
    public Money add(Money other) {
        if (!currency.equals(other.currency)) throw new IllegalArgumentException("currency mismatch");
        return new Money(amount.add(other.amount), currency);
    }
}

Takeaway: the constructor fixes the scale once (e.g. 2 for EUR, 0 for JPY), and RoundingMode.UNNECESSARY makes any accidental precision loss throw instead of silently rounding.

The alternative is storing minor units as a long (12.34 EUR = 1234 cents):

  • long minor units — best for high-volume ledgers and hot paths: exact, fast, compact, trivially summable in SQL. Works when the scale is fixed and you only add/subtract.
  • BigDecimal Money — best when you multiply by rates, percentages or prices with more decimal places (FX, interest, fees), because intermediate results need extra precision before a final, explicit rounding step.

Many trading systems combine both: long in storage and messaging, BigDecimal inside calculation code.

questions.question: How should money be stored in a relational database and transported over JSON APIs? Discuss NUMERIC/DECIMAL vs FLOAT columns, why JSON numbers are dangerous for amounts, and where rounding should (and should not) happen in a multi-step calculation pipeline.
questions.explanation:
In the database, money goes into NUMERIC/DECIMAL with explicit precision and scale, e.g. NUMERIC(19, 4). These are exact base-10 types. FLOAT/DOUBLE PRECISION are binary floating point and cannot represent values like 0.10 exactly — sums drift and reconciliation breaks.

Over JSON, the trap is that a JSON number is typically parsed into an IEEE-754 double by JavaScript and many default parsers, silently corrupting large or high-precision amounts. So send amounts as strings, with the currency alongside:

{ "amount": "1234.5600", "currency": "EUR" }

Takeaway: "1234.5600" survives every parser byte-for-byte; 1234.56 only survives by luck.

Rounding in a multi-step pipeline:

  • Keep full precision through intermediate steps (rate lookups, percentage fees, pro-rating) — rounding early loses cents that never come back.
  • Round once, at the boundary: when the value becomes an official amount someone is charged, booked or shown — with an explicitly chosen RoundingMode (e.g. HALF_EVEN for accounting).
  • Never let rounding happen implicitly (double arithmetic, DB column narrower than the value, default parser behavior) — every rounding should be a visible line of code.

questions.question: How does Spring Boot auto-configuration work under the hood? What roles do @EnableAutoConfiguration, conditional annotations (@ConditionalOnClass etc.) and starters play?
questions.explanation:
Auto-configuration is not magic — it is a pile of ordinary @Configuration classes that ship inside spring-boot-autoconfigure and switch themselves on or off based on what is on your classpath.

The chain: @SpringBootApplication contains @EnableAutoConfiguration, which reads the file META-INF/spring/org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.AutoConfiguration.imports and imports every class listed there. Each imported class is guarded by conditions:

@AutoConfiguration
@ConditionalOnClass(DataSource.class)          // only if JDBC is on the classpath
@EnableConfigurationProperties(DataSourceProperties.class)
public class DataSourceAutoConfiguration {

    @Bean
    @ConditionalOnMissingBean                  // only if YOU didn't define one
    DataSource dataSource(DataSourceProperties props) {
        return props.initializeDataSourceBuilder().build();
    }
}

So adding spring-boot-starter-data-jpa (a starter = just a curated dependency bundle, no code) puts Hibernate and a JDBC driver on the classpath, the @ConditionalOnClass checks pass, and a DataSource + EntityManagerFactory appear — but the moment you declare your own bean, @ConditionalOnMissingBean makes the auto-configured one back off.

Takeaway: starters trigger conditions, conditions gate plain @Configuration classes, and your own beans always win.

questions.question: How does @Transactional work in Spring? Explain the proxy mechanism, why self-invocation breaks it, and what propagation levels like REQUIRED and REQUIRES_NEW mean.
questions.explanation:
@Transactional works through a proxy: Spring wraps your bean in a proxy object that opens/commits/rolls back the transaction around the real method. That's why self-invocation breaks it — this.otherMethod() calls the raw object directly, bypassing the proxy, so otherMethod's transaction settings are ignored.

All propagation levels (what to do about an existing transaction):

  • REQUIRED (default) — join the current transaction, or start one if none exists.
  • REQUIRES_NEW — suspend the current one, run in a brand-new transaction (commits/rolls back independently — e.g. audit logs that must survive a rollback).
  • NESTED — savepoint inside the current transaction; inner rollback rolls back only to the savepoint.
  • SUPPORTS — join if present, otherwise run non-transactionally.
  • NOT_SUPPORTED — suspend any transaction, run without one.
  • MANDATORY — a transaction must already exist, else throw.
  • NEVER — a transaction must NOT exist, else throw.

Isolation levels (a separate attribute — how concurrent transactions see each other's data): READ_UNCOMMITTED (dirty reads possible), READ_COMMITTED (no dirty reads; default in Postgres/Oracle), REPEATABLE_READ (same row reads same twice; default in MySQL), SERIALIZABLE (full isolation, most locking), plus DEFAULT = use the database's own default. Propagation answers 'whose transaction am I in?'; isolation answers 'what can concurrent transactions see?'.

questions.question: Describe the lifecycle of a Spring bean from instantiation to destruction. Where do BeanPostProcessors, @PostConstruct and aware-interfaces fit in, and how does AOP proxying hook into this lifecycle?
questions.explanation:
A bean's life is a fixed pipeline, and knowing the order explains where every hook fits:

  1. Instantiate — constructor runs (constructor injection happens here).
  2. Populate — field/setter dependencies injected.
  3. Aware callbacks — BeanNameAware, ApplicationContextAware etc. hand the bean infrastructure references.
  4. BeanPostProcessor.postProcessBeforeInitialization — every BPP sees every bean.
  5. Init callbacks — @PostConstruct, then InitializingBean.afterPropertiesSet(), then init-method.
  6. BeanPostProcessor.postProcessAfterInitialization — this is where AOP proxies are created: the post-processor returns a proxy INSTEAD of your object, and that proxy is what everyone else gets injected.
  7. Bean is in use.
  8. Destruction — @PreDestroy, then DisposableBean.destroy(), then destroy-method (on context shutdown; prototype beans are never destroyed by Spring).
@Component
public class Cache implements InitializingBean {
    @PostConstruct  void warmUp()  { }   // runs FIRST
    public void afterPropertiesSet() { } // runs SECOND
    @PreDestroy     void flush()   { }   // on shutdown
}

Takeaway: BeanPostProcessors bracket the init callbacks, and because AOP proxying happens in the after-init hook, anything that grabs the raw bean earlier (e.g. inside the constructor) bypasses transactions and other AOP behavior.

questions.question: What is the difference between @Component, @Service, @Repository and @Controller? Do they behave differently at runtime, and what extra behavior does @Repository actually add?
questions.explanation:
All four stereotypes are meta-annotated with @Component, so component scanning treats them identically for bean creation. The differences show up in what OTHER machinery keys off them:

  • @Component — generic bean, no extra behavior.
  • @Service — pure documentation of the business layer; adds nothing at runtime (today).
  • @Repository — activates exception translation: PersistenceExceptionTranslationPostProcessor proxies the bean and converts raw persistence exceptions into Spring's DataAccessException hierarchy.
  • @Controller — marks the bean for Spring MVC, which scans only these for @RequestMapping handler methods.
@Repository
public class UserDao {
    void save(User u) { ... }
    // Hibernate throws ConstraintViolationException (Hibernate-specific)
    // caller receives DataIntegrityViolationException (Spring, vendor-neutral)
}

Takeaway: the stereotype tells Spring's infrastructure how to post-process the bean — @Repository translates exceptions, @Controller registers web handlers — so using the right one is functional, not just stylistic.

questions.question: Offset-based pagination (page=5&size=20) misbehaves on large, frequently-changing datasets. Why, and how does cursor (keyset) pagination fix it?
questions.explanation:
Intuition: page=5&size=20 means "skip 100 rows, give me the next 20". Two things go wrong: the database must still walk and discard those 100 rows (at page 5000 that's 100,000 rows scanned for 20 returned — cost grows with the offset), and the offset counts whatever the data looks like right now, so concurrent inserts/deletes shift the pages under the user.

-- user is reading page 5 (rows 101-120), sorted newest-first
-- meanwhile 3 new rows are inserted at the top
-- 'page=6' now skips 120 rows of the SHIFTED list:
--   3 rows the user already saw appear again (duplicates)
-- if 3 rows were DELETED instead, 3 unseen rows are silently skipped

Cursor (keyset) pagination replaces "skip N" with "continue after the last row I saw". The server returns an opaque cursor encoding the sort-key values of the last item; the next request filters on it:

SELECT * FROM posts
WHERE (created_at, id) < (:last_created_at, :last_id)  -- resume after cursor
ORDER BY created_at DESC, id DESC
LIMIT 20;
  • Fast: with an index on (created_at, id) the DB seeks straight to the cursor — no rows scanned and thrown away, page 1 and page 5000 cost the same.
  • Stable: the anchor is a concrete row, not a count — inserts/deletes elsewhere can't cause duplicates or skips.
  • Tie-breaker matters: id is added because created_at alone isn't unique — a non-unique cursor can skip or repeat rows on the boundary.

The trade-off: you lose "jump to page 37" and total page counts — you can only walk forward (or backward with a reversed cursor). That's why keyset fits infinite-scroll feeds and APIs (GitHub, Stripe, Slack all use cursors), while offset survives in small admin tables where random page access matters more than correctness.

questions.question: Where would you add caching in a typical web system, and what are the main strategies for keeping a cache and its underlying database from drifting apart?
questions.explanation:
Caching can sit at every hop between the user and the database — each layer absorbs traffic so the layers behind it see less:

Browser cache → CDN (static assets) → App server
                                        ├─ local in-memory cache (per instance)
                                        ├─ shared cache: Redis / Memcached
                                        └─ Database (+ its own buffer pool)

The most common application pattern is cache-aside (lazy loading):

value = redis.get(key)
if value is None:                 # cache miss
    value = db.query(...)
    redis.set(key, value, ttl=300)
return value

Keeping cache and DB from drifting apart — the main strategies:

  • TTL (expiration): every entry dies after N seconds. Simplest; bounds staleness but never eliminates it.
  • Invalidate on write: after a DB update, delete the cache key (deleting beats updating — the next read repopulates; concurrent updates can't leave a stale value behind).
  • Write-through: writes go to cache and DB together — cache is always warm and consistent, at the cost of write latency.
  • Write-behind: write to cache, flush to DB asynchronously — fast writes, but you can lose data on a crash.
StrategyStalenessComplexity
TTL onlyup to TTLtrivial
TTL + delete-on-writeseconds (race windows)low — the usual default
Write-through~nonemedium, slower writes
Watch out — cache stampede: when a hot key expires, thousands of requests miss at once and hammer the DB together. Mitigate with jittered TTLs, a per-key lock so only one request recomputes, or refreshing hot keys ahead of expiry.
Takeaway: cache-aside + sensible TTL + delete-on-write covers 90% of systems. Perfect consistency is not the goal — bounded, acceptable staleness is.

questions.question: What is gRPC and how does it fundamentally differ from a typical REST/JSON API? Cover the contract, the wire format and how client code is produced.
questions.explanation:
