gRPC is a remote-procedure-call framework: instead of hand-crafting HTTP requests to URL paths, the client calls what looks like a local method and gRPC handles the transport. Three fundamental differences from REST/JSON:

  • Contract-first: a .proto file is the single source of truth for services, methods and message shapes — unlike REST, where an OpenAPI spec is optional and often drifts.
  • Wire format: Protocol Buffers, a compact binary encoding, instead of human-readable JSON text — smaller payloads, much cheaper to parse.
  • Generated code: protoc generates typed client stubs and server skeletons in each language, so calling a remote service is a compile-checked method call, not a hand-built HTTP request.
service UserService {
  rpc GetUser (GetUserRequest) returns (User);
}
// client code after generation:
User u = stub.getUser(GetUserRequest.newBuilder().setId(42).build());

It runs over HTTP/2, which also enables streaming in both directions — something plain REST doesn't model.

questions.question: What are Protocol Buffers? Explain what a `.proto` file defines, why the binary encoding is smaller and faster than JSON, and what role code generation plays in the developer workflow.
questions.explanation:
Protocol Buffers is the schema language and binary serialization format gRPC uses. A .proto file defines message types (fields with types and numbers) and service interfaces:

message User {
  int64  id    = 1;
  string email = 2;
}

Why binary beats JSON on the wire:

  • Field numbers, not names, are sent — "email": costs 8+ bytes in JSON, ~1 byte in protobuf.
  • Values are packed natively: integers as compact varints, no quoting, no whitespace, no text→number parsing.
  • Decoding is a fast, schema-driven read, not generic string parsing — typically several times faster with payloads a fraction of the size.

Code generation closes the loop: protoc turns the .proto into typed classes plus client/server stubs in each language, so both sides share one contract, serialization is invisible, and a schema mismatch is a compile error rather than a runtime surprise.

questions.question: gRPC runs on HTTP/2. Which HTTP/2 features does it rely on — explain multiplexing, long-lived connections and header compression, and why gRPC couldn't be built the same way on HTTP/1.1.
questions.explanation:
gRPC leans on three HTTP/2 features:

  • Multiplexing: one TCP connection carries many concurrent streams, with frames from different calls interleaved. Each gRPC call is one stream, so 100 parallel RPCs share a single connection without waiting on each other.
  • Long-lived connections: the connection stays open and streams stay open as long as needed — which is exactly what server/client/bidirectional streaming RPCs are: one HTTP/2 stream that both sides keep writing messages into.
  • Header compression (HPACK): repeated metadata (method path, auth tokens, content-type) is sent once and then referenced by index, so per-call overhead is a few bytes.

On HTTP/1.1 none of this works: one request-per-connection at a time (head-of-line blocking, so clients open ~6 connections and still queue), no way to interleave frames, headers resent in full as plain text every time, and a strict request→response shape with no true bidirectional streaming. gRPC also uses HTTP/2 trailers to send the final status after a streamed body — another thing HTTP/1.1 handles poorly.

questions.question: Describe the four gRPC method types: unary, server streaming, client streaming and bidirectional streaming. Give a realistic use case for each.
questions.explanation:
The four method types differ in whether each side sends one message or a stream:

  • Unary — one request, one response; the RPC equivalent of a normal API call. Use case: GetUser(id) returns (User) — fetch a profile, create an order.
  • Server streaming — one request, a stream of responses. Use case: subscribe to live price updates, or download a large result set row-by-row instead of one giant payload.
  • Client streaming — a stream of requests, one final response. Use case: an IoT device uploading a batch of sensor readings, or chunked file upload, answered with one summary/ack.
  • Bidirectional streaming — both sides read and write independently on one open call. Use case: chat, multiplayer game state sync, or live speech-to-text where audio flows up while transcripts flow down.
rpc GetUser   (GetUserRequest)     returns (User);
rpc Watch     (WatchRequest)       returns (stream Event);
rpc Upload    (stream Chunk)       returns (UploadStatus);
rpc Chat      (stream ChatMessage) returns (stream ChatMessage);

questions.question: Why are field numbers in protobuf messages so important? Explain the rules for evolving a schema safely: what you may add, what you must never change, and what `reserved` is for.
questions.explanation:
On the wire protobuf sends field numbers, not names — string email = 2; is encoded as "field 2: ...". The number IS the field's identity, so old and new binaries can talk as long as the numbers keep meaning the same thing. Names are just local decoration; numbers are the contract.

Safe-evolution rules:

  • May add: new fields with new, never-used numbers. Old readers skip unknown fields; new readers see default values from old senders.
  • May do freely: rename a field (the name isn't on the wire).
  • Never: change an existing field's number or type, or reuse a deleted field's number — an old binary would decode the new data as the old meaning, silently corrupting values.
  • reserved makes deletion safe: it forbids future reuse at compile time.
message User {
  reserved 3;               // was: string nickname = 3;
  reserved "nickname";
  int64  id    = 1;
  string email = 2;
  string phone = 4;         // new field, fresh number
}

questions.question: How does HTTP caching work? Explain the roles of Cache-Control, ETag and Last-Modified, and describe what a conditional request (304 Not Modified) flow looks like.
questions.explanation:
HTTP caching has two independent mechanisms: freshness (how long may I reuse this without asking?) and validation (it's stale — is my copy still good?).

Cache-Control sets freshness and who may cache:

DirectiveMeaning
max-age=3600fresh for 1h — reuse without any request
no-cachemay store, but must revalidate before every use
no-storenever store anything (sensitive data)
public / privateshared caches (CDN) allowed / browser only

Validators travel with the response: ETag is an opaque content fingerprint (hash/version); Last-Modified is a timestamp (1-second resolution, weaker). The client echoes them back in a conditional request:

1. GET /logo.png
   ◀ 200 OK
     Cache-Control: max-age=3600
     ETag: "abc123"
     (body: 250 KB)

   ... 1h passes — the copy is now stale ...

2. GET /logo.png
   If-None-Match: "abc123"        ← "only send it if it changed"
   ◀ 304 Not Modified              ← headers only, NO body
     Cache-Control: max-age=3600   ← freshness clock restarts

   → browser reuses its 250 KB copy; one cheap round-trip instead of a re-download

The timestamp equivalent is If-Modified-Since: <date> against Last-Modified; when both are present, ETag wins.

Takeaway: max-age avoids the request entirely; ETag + 304 avoids the body when you must ask. The classic combo for static assets: hashed filenames + max-age=31536000, immutable; for HTML/APIs: no-cache + ETag.
